observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
The Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant (MAcco) to provide a reconciliation of noted variance in the other structures account along with the account’s related accumulated depreciation account and the latter to adjust the books for such variance; and
The MAcco to reclassify the error to its appropriate PPE account.

We recommended that the MAcco make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the misstatements in the DRR Equipment and Motor Vehicles accounts, ensuring that the FS accurately reflect the true balances as of year-end.

We recommended the following to the MAcco:
Adjust the books with the guidance of the following entry:
Representation Expenses		   ₱1,559,047.00
Donations				        963,500.00
Rent Expense				          842,000.00
Prizes					         425,984.00
Repairs and Maintenance Expense         139,750.00
Other MOE				             ₱3,930,281.00; and
Ensure that expenses are charged in their appropriate accounts and avoid the instance of charging items to Other MOE account when the same can be specifically categorized under specific expense accounts.

Adjust the GF books with the guidance of the following compound entry:
Accounts Payable		 	  2,575,996.35
Construction in Progress				  1,493,518.44
Due to Officers and Employees				   1,082,477.91;
Unpaid due to contractors must be recognized as Accounts Payable based on the progress billings as shown in the SWA and not based on obligation; and
Reconcile the aging and FS balance of the Accounts Payable account.

We recommended that the MAcco prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to accurately reflect the correct balance of the Due to NGAs account, as well as the Due to GOCCs and Trust Liabilities accounts. Additionally, we recommended that the MAcco ensure adherence to the proper classification of accounts, in strict compliance with Section 4 (7) of PD No. 1445 and the RCA for LGUs.

We recommended that the MAcco ensure proper recording of liability accounts based on their nature and purpose in accordance with LGCOA, and to adjust the TF books with the guidance of the following compound entry:
Trust Liabilities					840,000.00
Due to NGAs									840,000.00
Further, we also recommended to exert effort in identifying the source of the ₱500,000.00 transfer and reflect in books its proper accounting treatment, otherwise, the amount shall be reverted to the General Fund as Miscellaneous Income and be appropriated for the PPAs of the Municipality since the National Treasury does not accept return/remittance thereof if the original source agency cannot be identified.

We recommended the following:
The Inventory Committee and the MAcco to provide a reconciliation of noted variance in the other structures account along with the account’s related accumulated depreciation account and the latter to adjust the books for such variance;
The Management to investigate if upon reconciliation of the Inventory Committee and MAcco proves that indeed the assets were lost and determine the liability of those who are accountable; and
The MAcco to reclassify the error to its appropriate PPE account.

We recommended that the MAcco make the necessary adjusting entries to present the correct balances of Due to LGUs and other liability accounts affected and adhere strictly to the RCA for LGUs to ensure compliance with proper accounting and to maintain the integrity and transparency of financial reporting.

We recommended that Management instruct the MAcco, the MT and the Inventory Committee to immediately reconcile their records and effect the necessary adjustments on the affected accounts in compliance with pertinent provisions of the NGAS for LGUs.
We recommended the following:
The MAcco and General Service Officer to reconcile the land account per books and per records and trace the existing certificate of titles with the recorded ones for accurate valuation of land account. After which, the MAcco record the existing certificates of titles not recorded in the books pursuant to IPSAS; and
The Management with respect to unregistered parcels of land to prioritize the registration and transfer of the title of land in the name of LGU to have conclusive evidence of ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in adherence to Section 39 (2) of PD No. 1445 and Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20 1992.
We recommended the MAcco to reclassify the errors to their appropriate PPE accounts as guided by the following:

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately transfer the cash back-up of the unexpended balances of 5% LDRRM Fund (QRF and MF-MOOE) from the GF account to the TF account in order to fairly present the CIB-LCCA account and undertake the same in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. Likewise, we recommended the MAcco to comply with the prescribed accounting entries for the unexpended QRF and MF-MOOE from Current Appropriation of the 5% LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the MAcco make the necessary disclosures of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF under the account TL-DRRMF in the Notes to FS to comply with COA Circular No. 2012-12 dated September 12, 2012. Likewise, we recommended that all concerned employees prepare and submit the required reports as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014.

We recommended the MAcco to reconcile the “Due from Other Funds” and “Due to Other Funds” accounts and make the necessary adjusting entries to correct the balances and submit proofs of the same to the Office of the Auditor. Likewise, we recommended her to undertake regular and timely review and reconciliation of the two (2) reciprocal accounts in compliance to the above-mentioned rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer observe the prescribed due dates for the remittances of withheld taxes, premium contributions and loans in compliance to the aforementioned BIR, Pag-IBIG, PhilHealth and GSIS laws, rules and regulations in order to avoid incurrence of possible penalties.
Also, we recommended that Management identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and immediately coordinate with the GSIS to reconcile and update agency/employee records.
In addition, we commended the efforts of the Municipal Accounting Office in resolving the above-mentioned reconciling items and encourage them to continue identifying the cause of discrepancies as well as locating all available data and supporting documents of the remaining unreconciled items in the SLs tagged as “For Recon” to come up with a more accurate and reliable information.

We recommended the following to Management:
Ensure the application of the alternative method of procurement, i.e. Direct Retail Purchase, of non-bulk Petroleum Fuel, Oil and Lubricant products, otherwise, undertake the same through Competitive Bidding. Submit to the Office of the Auditor pertinent reports and documents in relation to this initiative;
Strictly abide by the procedures and controls outlined in COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 prescribing the use of the Manual of Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicle; and
Enjoin personnel/s delegated to directly purchase or contract POL products to provide documents showing proofs of evaluation and finally contracting the above-named gasoline stations as supplier to the LGU.
Likewise, we recommended the MAcco to ensure completeness of supporting documents prior to processing of Disbursement Vouchers.

We recommended Management to furnish to the Office of the Auditor its legal basis which authorizes it to disburse the limited funds of the government for SEI to its JOs and COS. Absence of which, the total disbursements of ₱2,891,592.00 shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management submit a report on the extraordinary suggestion, invention, superior accomplishment and other personal effects of each individual who received the PRAISE Incentive Bonus including the computation of the savings generated or realized therefrom in which the Municipality has been benefited.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate refund of the medical allowance paid to its municipal officials and regular employees in violation of the RA No. 6758 and submit proof of refunds thereof to the Audit Team for validation. Likewise, we recommended that Management only grant allowances and benefits authorized under the Act, subject to funds availability. Further, such other additional compensation not otherwise specified in RA No. 6758 may be granted as may be determined by the DBM.

We recommended that Management cause the refund of the Anniversary Bonus paid to the nineteen (19) employees who were ineligible for the grant that is in violation of DBM LBC No. 65 dated March 03, 1997 and submit proof of refunds thereof, otherwise, Notice of Disallowance shall be issued wherein the recipients, as well as the officials who approved the payment, shall be jointly liable.

We recommended that Management furnish a written explanation addressing the identified irregularities in payment of CNA Incentive in the form of CNA Signing Bonus and for its failure to enact an Appropriation Ordinance covering the Supplemental Budget for the payment of said Incentives which resulted to non-review by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of its legality and propriety. Failure to provide such may result in the audit disallowance of the mentioned benefits, given the deficiency in the legality and validity of Sangguniang Bayan Resolution No. 2023-310 dated December 18, 2023.

We recommended the following:
The LCE, in collaboration with the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer and the Sangguniang Bayan, to establish clear-cut guidelines and procedures for granting and disbursing donations. This should be done with thorough consideration of the social and living conditions of recipients to ensure a fair, just, and equitable distribution of funds;
Management and the Local Sanggunian to recalibrate its existing Ordinance on Educational Assistance to ensure that they include reasonable and transparent criteria for the distribution of educational assistance. This may involve establishing clear guidelines on the amounts allocated based on factors such as financial need, academic performance, or other relevant criteria; and
Management to provide its written justification on the legality and reasonableness of the expenditures totaling ₱49,000.00 for evaluation in audit and stop granting financial assistance intended for private purposes and failure to do so shall result of these expenditures to audit disallowances.

We recommended that Management compute and enforce the liquidated damages against the defaulting Contractor and submit proof of payment thereof together with the Statement of Work Accomplished as of the expiry date, December 22, 2022 for Project I and January 15, 2023 for Project VII, for validation. Likewise, we recommended Management to collect from the defaulting Contractor of Project II the computed liquidated damages of ₱15,597.39, or if same was already collected, provide proof of settlement to the Office of the Auditor.
Further, we recommended that Management require the Contractor of Project V to immediately rectify the noted scaling defects with corresponding amount of ₱127,841.08, otherwise, the amount shall be disallowed in audit. Proofs of rectification by the Contractor must be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for submission to the COA Regional Office as basis for re-inspection/revalidation.

We recommended Management to submit its written justification why the amount of ₱345,000.00 should not be disallowed in audit. Likewise, we recommended the MAcco to furnish the Office of the Auditor the required “Certificate from HRO/U that the claimants have not incurred more than 50 days leave without pay within the preceding 5-year period, as the case may be, or proof of eligibility to receive incentive/service recognition” and proofs of satisfactory services of the nine (9) eligible municipal employees to support payment of their Loyalty Incentives and for further evaluation in audit.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the MAcco and Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their records to establish the correct balances;
Provide a written justification as to the improper utilization of the 70% MF for evaluation in audit; and
Strictly and prudently comply with all the rules and regulations laid out relative to LDRRM Fund.
We recommended the LCE the following:
Direct the MAcco to ascertain that future disbursements of RATA are substantiated with all the necessary documentations; and
Enjoin the Municipal Officials and Heads of Offices to promptly furnish the Office of the Auditor the required supporting documents to substantiate the RATA disbursed in year 2023. Failure to comply may result in the issuance of a Notice of Suspension.
Further, we recommended that Management require the Municipal Officials and Heads of Offices to specify the (1) type of leave applied for in a particular day on their Daily Time Records and (2) mode of transportation on their Travel Orders to facilitate the pre-audit by the MAcco on the propriety and validity of their future RATA claims.

We recommended that Management:
Provide a written justification as to the improper utilization of the 70% MF for evaluation in audit;
Revisit the Job Order Workers’ contract to ensure that all services required and delivered are DRRM-related; and
Strictly and prudently comply with all the rules and regulations laid out relative to LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the prescribed period of payment of Uniform or Clothing Allowance which is the month of April as explicitly stated in the aforesaid DBM issuances in order to avoid future audit disallowances.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to coordinate with the League of Municipalities of Nueva Vizcaya and demand for the issuance of government prescribed official receipts (Accountable Form No.51) for all disbursements to the League of Municipalities of the Philippines (LMP)-Nueva Vizcaya Chapter to fully establish financial accountability and to protect public funds to proper use.

We recommended the following:
As a withholding agent, Management to ensure that taxes be withheld on payment of BAC honoraria and promptly remit to the Bureau of Internal Revenue said taxes withheld within the prescribed period to avoid penalties, surcharges and interests which if in any case of late remittance, it shall be personally borne by the defaulting LGU officials concerned;
The BAC to submit the required supporting documents enumerated in the COA Circular for the disbursements of BAC honoraria made in the year 2023 for further evaluation; and
The MAcco to ascertain the completeness of the documents required by COA Circular No. 2023-004 prior to processing of payment of BAC honoraria to avoid audit suspension or disallowance, if warranted. Likewise, proper accounting of the transaction must be observed to present fairly the FSs.

We recommended Management to ensure full liquidation of all cash advances at year-end through the submission of liquidation reports along with the requisite supporting documents, or by refunding any remaining balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002 to avoid accumulation of cash advances and mitigate the potential risk of loss through theft or misappropriation of funds. Likewise, we recommended Management to demand immediate liquidation of cash advances long outstanding or withhold salaries of the Accountable Officers as prescribed in Section 5.9 of the Circular.

We recommended to Management that the appropriated amounts of the 20% DF be fully utilized for the implementation and accomplishments of the lined-up projects contained in the approved Annual Investment Plan in order to optimally attain its desired benefits to the advantage of its constituents.

We recommended that the Gender Focal Point System Officer ensure the annual preparation of the GAD Plan using the required format in its form including the “Cause of Gender Issue” and “Source of Fund” columns which will be the basis of the GAD budget in the Local Expenditure Program and submit the same to the office of the auditor. This could be facilitated by including the element of Institutionalizing GAD Database/Sex-disaggregated Data which could show a holistic picture of the organization with respect to implementation of a gender- responsive institution. Such could be done through survey as data-gathering tools and summarized for basis of GAD plan preparation.
We recommended that the Gender Focal Point System Officer ensure compliance with the required format of the GAD AR as presented in Annex B of PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01 and prepare the necessary attachments for complete support of such accomplishments.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to provide a written justification as to the improper utilization of the 30% QRF considering the proximity of the dates of the outbreak, the provincial declaration and release of the financial assistance; and
Management, through the MDRRM Officer, to observe the provisions of Section 21 of RA 10121 or Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010.

We recommended that Management adhere to the funding requirement of one percent of its IRA to support its LCPC, as mandated by Section 15 of RA No. 9344. Upon allocation, it is essential to guarantee the effective implementation of priority plans for children, such as those outlined in Item II of DILG MC No. 2012-150.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the PS Limitation prescribed under Section 325 (a) of RA No. 7160 and shall ensure that the actual expenses for one fiscal year must not exceed the required 45 percent PS Limitation to avoid audit disallowances.

We recommended the MAcco to strictly enforce the provisions pertaining to net take home pay as stipulated in the GAA to ensure that government personnel have enough funds to support for their daily needs until the next pay day.

We recommended the following:
The LO should immediately settle the remaining cash shortage by depositing the amount to the bank account of the LGU;
The LO must diligently verify all reports of collections turned over to her by the Collectors to ascertain the accuracy of the collections remitted and to properly establish accountability in her hand; and
The LO and MAcco should adjust the balance of accountability per cashbook and general ledger, respectively, to reflect the reconciled balance of accountability as of August 11, 2023 per cash examination.

We recommended that the Accounting maintain in sufficient detail its records to conveniently establish specific balances of accountabilities per account, and thereafter, the LO reconcile her cash book records with the accounting ledgers at least quarterly or as the need arises.
Moreover, we recommended that the discrepancies identified in the cash examination report be adjusted accordingly by the LO and the Accounting Office to come up with more accurate and reliable reports and cash balance.

We recommended that the Collectors stop the transfer of accountable forms to unbonded employee/s and strictly implement the rules and regulations in safeguarding the use of accountable forms and government funds pursuant to Section 86 (F), Chapter III, Book 2 of the LTOM, 2nd Edition and Section 102 (2) of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the LO and/or Collector concerned present to the Audit Team all unused Accountable Forms to properly account the ending balance on hand of the CRAAF as against the physical count of the unused AFs to properly monitor the accountabilities.
Furthermore, we recommended that the discrepancies identified in the CRAAF be adjusted accordingly by the LO and the MT to come up with more accurate and reliable report.

1.8	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare manual Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) in the e-ngas to correct the balance of Accumulated Depreciation-Road Networks and Government Equity accounts reported in the financial statements, in line with Section 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of P.D. 1445.
1.18	We recommended to Management the following:
the Municipal Engineer to review the physical status of projects aggregating to P9,561,397.45 and prepare the corresponding Certificates of Completion for approval of the Municipal Mayor and for submission to the Municipal Accountant as bases in recording transactions;
the Municipal Accountant to recognize the unbilled portion of the completed projects amounting to P5,552,248.88 as Accounts Payable and immediately reclassify the completed infrastructure projects amounting to P9,561,397.45 into the appropriate PPE accounts consistent with Paragraph 14 of IPSAS 17 and COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015; and
the Municipal Engineer to notify the concerned contractors to submit the necessary supporting documents for the processing of their claims on the unbilled portion of the completed projects, if any. Otherwise, the outstanding unliquidated accounts payable for two years or more shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund pursuant to Section 98 of PD 1445.
1.23	We recommended that Management thru the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassification of accounts, in line with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
1.31	We recommended to Management the following:
the Inventory Committee to rectify the submitted RPCPPE by providing all the information required such as the description of item, property number, unit measure, unit value, quantity per property card and per physical count, as well as the shortage/overage noted; and
the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to update and immediately reconcile their PPELCs and PCs to ensure the integrity of property custodianship and that adequate internal control is adapted in accordance with pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

2.8	We recommended that Management:
strictly comply with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Cir. No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance. The “No liquidation, no additional cash advance policy” must be strictly implemented;
require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances by issuing demand letter and if necessary withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees until the settlement thereof; and
the Municipal Accountant reclassify the accounts with negative balances caused by excess liquidations into Due to Officers and Employees.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Accountant initiate the filing of request for the write off of dormant receivable accounts by securing the necessary documents as per Section 8 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016. Also, we recommended Management to issue demand letters for the enforcement of collections to former officials and employees who have known residences, otherwise secure a certification of no residence in the barangay of last known address which could be one of the bases to write-off the accounts. Lastly, we recommended Management to exert diligent and wise efforts to verify and substantiate the other receivable accounts with no supporting documents in order to support its validity.

4.12	We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, consider the proposed adjusting entries in both the General and Trust Fund books of accounts consistent with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 to reflect a more accurate financial position and performance of LGU Kayapa.

5.9	We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Accountant, immediately conduct reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts and prepare necessary adjustments, if any, in line with Section 21 of IPSAS and Section 111 of P.D. 1445.

6.6	We recommended that Management strictly observe Section 48.3 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of RA 9184 in adopting changes in the method of procurement to ensure transparency and competitiveness in the procurement process.

7.5	We recommended that Management ensure that warranty security is required from the suppliers on procured goods, either in the form of retention money or special bank guarantee in an amount equivalent to at least one percent (1%) of the total contract price, in adherence to Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

8.9	We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist immediately submit the following documents:
Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) to support the issuance of inventories for distribution; and
the Acknowledgement Receipt by actual recipients along with their names, addresses and contact numbers to properly support the distribution of inventories to the intended beneficiaries.

9.5	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant henceforth record the procurement of fertilizer and other agricultural supplies intended to be distributed to farmers, as Inventories for Distribution instead of Inventories for Consumption to properly account for the purpose of these items and facilitate monitoring of the actual movement of the inventories from requisition up to the actual distribution to intended beneficiaries.

10.6	We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Budget Officer, prepare the Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations-DRRMF in accordance with the Section 7, Chapter 3, Volume I of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs to provide relevant and adequate information that are useful for decision making.

11.6	We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 on the withholding of taxes to help the government in generating revenues to meet its appropriation requirement and to avoid penalties and legal action against the erring withholding agents from the BIR.

We recommended that Management reconcile balance of the PPE account in the FS with that of the PPE balance per Physical Inventory Report for proper adjustment of the variance or difference noted. Reconciliation should be done regularly after each annual physical count of the PPE so that discrepancies noted will be properly adjusted for a fair presentation of the PPE balances in the FS at year-end.

Require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances and if necessary withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees until the settlement thereof.
Comply strictly with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Cir. No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

Intensify its efforts by instituting measures deemed appropriate to ensure collection of the long outstanding receivables. And take the necessary actions:
(a) Particularly the Local Chief Executive request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the Guidelines and Procedures on the Write-off of Dormant Receivable Accounts, Unliquidated Cash Advances, and Fund Transfers; and,
(b) Exert diligent effort to verify and substantiate the other receivable accounts amounting to ₱270.00 in order to support its validity.
(c) File the request for the write off of dormant receivable accounts as per Section 8 of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Accounting Office record the receipt and utilization of the BGCM in accordance with Section 128 in relation to Sections 38 a(3) and 120; and 161 of GAM for LGUs Vol. 1. Likewise, record the corresponding accounts involved according to their descriptions as embodied in the GAM for LGUs Vol. III. Finally, we propose the following entries in the receipt and utilization of the BGCM:

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit the cash on hand balance of the Municipality and ensure compliance with depositing intact his collections within the period set forth by manual to preserve municipal cash and to prevent the possible loss or misuse thereof.

We recommended that the accounting transfer the unspent balance of current year at year-end to reflect accurate financial position and performance of the Municipality to comply with the regulations and avoid that the unspent current year balance be recognized only the next accounting year to prevent errors in the accounting for DRRM Fund. Lastly, the accounting should reconcile the balances of the ledger of DRRM Fund Trust Liability account and the fund’s balance as disclosed in the Notes to financial statements and adjust the books accordingly.

We recommended that management record all purchase of goods to their respective inventory account following the perpetual inventory method, with exception of those purchase through petty cash for reliability of reporting of amount of inventory. Further, we recommended that the consumption and distribution of inventories be supported with Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and in cases of distribution of food assistance to constituents, the transaction should be supported with list of recipients and their acknowledgement of receipt of welfare goods.

We recommended that management refrain from charging expenditures against the 20% DF which are not allowable under the above-mentioned guidelines and always bear in mind that development projects partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures and Covid-19 related expenditure with issuance of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular 2020-01.

We recommended that Management comply with the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) Department Order (DO) No. 197-2016 in the computation of pay items of the construction projects in the detailed estimate as a basis for setting the Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC). Also, the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ascertain the accuracy and propriety of contractors’ Bill of Quantities in the latter’s bid such that OCM and/or PM are not charged to items which are not entitled to them, ensure in all bids that 5% VAT is charged and no unnecessary charges or mark-ups are added to all pay items.  Finally, we recommended that Management avoid the tampering of BOQ and that the details of the computation of OCM, Profit and VAT be presented always in the BOQ.

We recommended that Management review the variation orders and before approval, see to it that it is within the general scope of the project as bid and awarded and cost for additional work do not exceed 10% of the project cost and will not exceed the Approved Budget for the Contract.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer forward a copy of certified list of the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year based on the latter’s Real Property Tax Account Registers to the Municipal Accountant as a basis in setting-up deferred Special Education Tax and Special Education Tax Receivable.
observations.managementAction: No action taken.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

Require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances and if necessary withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees until the settlement thereof.

Comply strictly with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Cir. No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Some accounts remain unliquidated.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 3, Page 47 of this report.

We recommended that management:

observations.recommendation: 1.6	We recommended that Management reconcile the inventory count reflected in the inventory report and the balance of assets per books of accounts to permit fair presentation of PPE in the financial statements. Reconciliation should be done regularly after each physical count of the PPE so that discrepancies noted will be properly adjusted for a fair presentation of the PPE balances in the FS.

2.9	We recommended that Management process the transfer of ownership of the lots in the name of the LGU of Kayapa since there were already improvements incorporated in these lots. We further recommend that the Accounting Office record the lots not yet recognized in the books of accounts to present fairly the FS in all material respect.

3.7	We recommended that management:
Require/demand the immediate settlement of all long overdue cash advances and if necessary withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees until the settlement thereof.
Comply strictly with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Cir. No. 97 – 002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

4.11	We recommended that management:
Intensify its efforts by instituting measures deemed appropriate to ensure collection of these long outstanding receivables.
The Local Chief Executive may request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 as long as the documentary requirements necessary will be submitted together with the request for write-off.
Exert diligent effort to verify and substantiate the other receivable accounts amounting to ₱270.00 in order to support its validity.

5.4	We recommended that management adjust the procurement of medicines under the PhilHealth Capitation Fund at year end to reflect the actual inventory on hand for proper presentation of Drugs and Medicines Inventory account in the FS.
6.4	We recommended that management ensure that all budgeted projects, programs and activities are optimally and fully implemented.

7.5	We recommended that Management enforce measures to maintain the increasing trend in the collection of revenues to optimize the local taxing and revenue raising powers of the Municipality to fund government projects for the benefit of constituents.

8.5	We recommended that management fast track the implementation of all on – going and not yet started programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilization thereof and timely brings the desired benefits to the intended beneficiaries.

9.5	We recommended that management refrain from charging expenditures against the 20% DF which are not allowable under the above-mentioned guidelines and always bear in mind that development projects partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.

10.7	We recommended that management attached in the disbursement voucher the delivery and time schedule of all procurements and indicate in the Notice to Proceed and Contract the time duration for the delivery and implementation of all projects to aid the monitoring and evaluation of projects. See to it that documents relative/supporting the procurement are properly accomplished to be able to determine delivery dates, contract duration and/or completion time of projects.

11.6	We recommended that Management attached the delivery and completion schedule including approved time extension to implement the project, suspension of work and variation orders in the disbursement voucher to properly facilitate the monitoring of the delivery and implementation of projects. Further, we recommended that Management impose appropriately the rule on imposition of liquidated damages as stated in the Government Procurement Law to observe timely delivery and implementation of projects.

12.5	We recommended that Management explain the reason for the slippage and submit to the auditor any variation order or suspension of work to justify any change in the work or interruption in the implementation of the project and make sure to consider these before fully settling the amount due to the contractor. Further we recommended, Management to refrain from fully paying the contractor if the project was not completed. Likewise, we recommend that the contractor refund the overpaid amount to the Municipality.

13.6	We recommended that Management require the suppliers and contractors to post warranty security to cover possible expenses from manufacturing and structural defects. Management should also refrain from accepting Surety Bond as a substitute for retention money if the project does incur delay and is satisfactorily undertaken.

14.4	We recommended that Management require the suppliers and contractors post performance security before signing the contract to ensure faithful compliance by the suppliers and contractors of their contractual obligations and to recover certain amount in case of their default.

15.9	We recommended that Management attach complete documentary requirements on their procurements including construction contracts and those procurements not subject to public bidding. Lastly, we recommended that management should ensure that the contractor or supplier’s receipt date on NTP be indicated and also with the report date of SWA for proper monitoring of projects. Also, we recommended that Management require the suppliers and contractors to post performance and warranty securities to reduce the risk exposure of the Municipality. Lastly, we recommended that Management impose appropriately the rule on imposition of liquidated damages as stated in the Government Procurement Law to observe timely delivery and implementation of projects.

8.9	We recommended that cash advances be granted to the designated Disbursing Officer for proper monitoring and liquidation thereof. 9.4	We recommended that management use the DRRM Fund for expenditures involving disaster risk mitigation, preparedness and response to maximize the purpose of the fund.

10.6	We recommended that the concerned officials and LSB members of the municipality prepare and approve the SEF Budget for the ensuing year within the timeline as set forth in Section 5.5 of Deped-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 so that the fund would be utilized as planned or as needed.

11.6	We recommended that management evaluate the components of the fund balance maintained under its SEF account to determine the actual unexpended balance thereof and ensure its inclusion in the SEF budget in the ensuing year.

12.6	We recommended that the Management cause the completion of the sanitary landfill to avoid contamination in the area and to refrain from dumping of garbage in the unfinished sanitary landfill. Finally, we also recommended that the Municipality segregate recyclable wastes with residual ones before disposal which could be strengthened by “no collection, no segregation policy” or segregation at source as mandated by R.A. 9003.

13.5	We recommended that management fast tract the implementation of all on – going and not yet started programmed and funded projects under the 20% MDF to optimize utilisation thereof and timely brings the desired benefits to the intended beneficiaries.

15.5	We recommended that the Municipality utilize its available fund under the PFPR to procure medicines, medical supplies, equipment as well as improvement of its Rural Health Unit facilities.

16.4	We recommended that the Accountable Officer reconcile her cashbook with the Accounting ledgers at least quarterly. Discrepancies identified should be adjusted accordingly to come up with updated, accurate and reliable balance of accountabilities.
